title: Data Structures
description: An extensive guide on Python data structures: from built-in collections to linear (stacks, queues) and non-linear (BST, graphs) representations with traversals.

sections.title: Built-in Collections
sections.description: Python's built-in collections are highly optimized. Lists represent mutable dynamic arrays, Tuples represent immutable records, Sets represent unique collections, and Dictionaries represent key-value mappings.
sections.codeBlock:
# 1. Lists (Mutable ordered array)
nums = [1, 2, 3]
nums.append(4)
print(nums)            # [1, 2, 3, 4]

# 2. Tuples (Immutable ordered sequence)
point = (10, 20)
print(point[0])        # 10

# 3. Sets (Unordered unique collections)
unique_nums = {1, 2, 2, 3}
print(unique_nums)     # {1, 2, 3}

# 4. Dictionaries (Key-value ordered mappings)
user = {"name": "Alice", "role": "admin"}
print(user.get("age", 25)) # 25 (safe default value access)

sections.title: Linear Structures: Stacks & Queues
sections.description: Stacks follow LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) and are efficiently implemented using lists. Queues follow FIFO (First-In-First-Out) and should be implemented using collections.deque to avoid O(N) memory shifting on list pops.
sections.codeBlock:
# 1. Stack Implementation (LIFO)
stack = []
stack.append("A")      # Push
stack.append("B")
print(stack.pop())     # "B" (Pop)
print(stack)           # ["A"]

# 2. Queue Implementation (FIFO)
from collections import deque
queue = deque()
queue.append("X")      # Enqueue
queue.append("Y")
print(queue.popleft())  # "X" (Dequeue in O(1) time complexity)
print(list(queue))     # ["Y"]

sections.title: Non-Linear Structures: Binary Search Tree
sections.description: Trees represent hierarchical data. A Binary Search Tree (BST) maintains sorted nodes where left children are smaller than parent, and right children are larger. Traversal functions visit nodes in specific sequences.

sections.title: Non-Linear Structures: Graphs & Traversals
sections.description: Graphs model networks of interconnected nodes (vertices) and paths (edges). An adjacency list maps vertices to list of neighbors. Traversal techniques include Depth-First Search (DFS) and Breadth-First Search (BFS).
sections.codeBlock:
# Graph Adjacency List
graph = {
    'A': ['B', 'C'],
    'B': ['D', 'E'],
    'C': ['F'],
    'D': [],
    'E': ['F'],
    'F': []
}

# 1. Depth-First Search (DFS) - Recursive Stack
def dfs(node, visited, path):
    if node not in visited:
        visited.add(node)
        path.append(node)
        for neighbor in graph[node]:
            dfs(neighbor, visited, path)

visited_set = set()
dfs_path = []
dfs('A', visited_set, dfs_path)
print(dfs_path)        # ['A', 'B', 'D', 'E', 'F', 'C']

# 2. Breadth-First Search (BFS) - Iterative Queue
from collections import deque
def bfs(start_node):
    visited = {start_node}
    queue = deque([start_node])
    path = []
    while queue:
        node = queue.popleft()
        path.append(node)
        for neighbor in graph[node]:
            if neighbor not in visited:
                visited.add(neighbor)
                queue.append(neighbor)
    return path

bfs_path = bfs('A')
print(bfs_path)        # ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E', 'F']

details:
Core Collections Performance: Lists in CPython are dynamic arrays (storing pointers to objects), offering amortized O(1) inserts at the end but O(n) inserts or deletes at the beginning due to memory shifting. Tuples are fixed-size immutable lists, which are lighter on memory. Sets and Dictionaries rely on highly optimized hash tables. Dictionaries use the dynamic resizing hash map pattern with open addressing, preserving insertion order (since Python 3.6+). Sets are essentially dictionaries without values, ensuring unique elements and O(1) lookups.

Linear Structures: Stacks operate on LIFO principles, easily modeled by list's append() and pop(). However, using a list as a FIFO Queue (with pop(0)) is inefficient because it shifts all remaining elements in memory, causing O(n) overhead. Utilizing collections.deque (a doubly-linked list of blocks) provides O(1) appends and pops from both ends, making it the standard choice for queues and sliding window algorithms.

Hierarchical and Network Structures: Non-linear structures like Trees and Graphs are implemented using custom classes or standard mappings. Trees (e.g. Binary Search Trees) organize nodes hierarchically, achieving average-case O(log n) searches but potentially degrading to O(n) if unbalanced. Graphs are represented either by Adjacency Matrices (useful for dense graphs) or Adjacency Lists (highly efficient for sparse graphs). Depth-First Search (DFS) uses a stack (or recursion) to explore paths deeply, whereas Breadth-First Search (BFS) uses a queue to explore level-by-level, serving as the basis for finding the shortest path in unweighted graphs.
